note: Most accessible palace in Korea; ramps everywhere, free loaner wheelchairs/strollers, free electric carts around the vast grounds, accessible restrooms, and the hourly Changing of the Guard is viewable seated. Palace buildings/throne hall not enterable; one cobbled courtyard is rough.
Beautiful traditional hanok neighbourhood, but genuinely hilly - a power chair manages the inclines, a manual chair needs assistance. Worth it for the architecture; go with a companion.
Seoul's 'Times Square' shopping/street-food district; well-cared-for sidewalks, streets closed to cars by day, most stores accessible. Flat and easy, gets crowded.
Former presidential residence, now open; smooth well-maintained grounds, no steps at entrance, elevator between the two floors inside. Surprisingly accommodating.
On the Gyeongbokgung grounds; accessible with elevators/ramps, showcasing Korean folk life. Good pairing with the palace and a solid rainy-day option.
Zaha Hadid's futuristic landmark; modern, fully accessible with lifts and smooth flowing floors. Design shows and night lighting.

Iconic hilltop tower with city views; reachable and the tower has elevators to the observation deck. Getting up Namsan is easiest via accessible options (some cable-car/transfer steps) - check on the day.
Riverside parks with wide flat paved paths, bike/roll lanes, picnic lawns and cruise options; relaxed and stroller-friendly. Great for families.
Traditional arts-and-crafts street; the main pedestrianized stretch is largely flat and navigable, though some galleries/tea houses have steps. Great for souvenirs.
UNESCO palace with the famous Secret Garden; main courtyards partly accessible via ramps but the Secret Garden and some areas have slopes/gravel/steps. Less accessible than Gyeongbokgung.
